name: Pause Push-Up
desc: Set up in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your chest all the way to the floor and pause there for 2 full seconds without resting your weight on the ground. Keep everything tight — core, glutes, quads. Then press back up explosively. The pause eliminates the stretch reflex, forcing your muscles to generate force from a dead stop. This makes every rep significantly harder than a regular push-up.

name: Slow Eccentric Push-Up
desc: Begin in standard push-up position. Lower your chest to the floor over exactly 5 seconds — counting one-one-thousand, two-one-thousand in your head. At the bottom, press back up normally. The 5-second lowering phase is called the eccentric phase and it creates the most muscle damage (the good kind) which drives growth. You will need significantly less reps to achieve the same fatigue as regular push-ups.

imgSearch: slow push up eccentric exercise

name: Explosive Push-Up (Clapping)
desc: Start in push-up position. Lower your chest toward the floor under control, then press up with maximum explosive force so your hands leave the ground. Clap your hands together in mid-air, then land with slightly bent elbows to absorb the impact. This plyometric version builds explosive pushing power and athleticism. The landing phase is crucial — never lock your elbows on landing.

name: Staggered Push-Up
desc: Set up in push-up position but place one hand forward of your shoulder and one hand at shoulder level, making a staggered stance. Lower yourself down and press back up. This uneven loading creates asymmetric tension across your chest and shoulders. Alternate which hand is forward each set or every rep. This is a step toward one-arm push-up mastery as it shifts more load to one side.

name: Hindu Push-Up
desc: Start in downward dog position — hips high, body forming an inverted V. Swoop your chest forward and down in an arc, passing just above the floor, then push up into upward dog with hips low and chest high. Reverse the movement to return to downward dog. This ancient Indian wrestling exercise combines a push-up with a full spinal wave and hits chest, shoulders, triceps and back in one fluid movement. It improves shoulder mobility while building strength simultaneously.

name: Dive Bomber Push-Up
desc: Start in downward dog. Keeping hips high, sweep your nose toward the floor in a dive bomber arc going forward with chest low to the ground then pushing up to cobra position. Unlike the Hindu push-up, do NOT reverse the movement — instead go back the same way you came, reversing the arc. This means you do the same arc twice per rep, doubling the shoulder and chest work.

name: Tiger Bend Push-Up
desc: From a forearm plank position, press up one arm at a time to a push-up position using just your triceps and shoulders — no momentum, no swinging. Then lower back to forearm plank one arm at a time. This is an elite calisthenics movement that requires extraordinary tricep, shoulder and core strength. It is a precursor to the forearm stand and is used by gymnasts and advanced calisthenics athletes. Take years not weeks to build to this.

name: Inverted Row (Table Row Variation)
desc: Lie under a sturdy table with hands gripping the edge, body completely straight from head to heel and the table directly above your chest. Your body should be at a challenging angle — the more horizontal you are, the harder it is. Pull your chest up to the table by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Hold for 1 second at the top, then lower with full control. This is one of the best back exercises available without a pull-up bar.

name: Towel Door Row
desc: Loop a towel around the door handle of a closed door. Stand facing the door, hold one end of the towel in each hand, and lean back until your arms are extended. Pull yourself toward the door by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades. This is an incredibly accessible pulling exercise that can be done in any room. name: Floor Back Extension
desc: Lie face down with your hands placed lightly behind your head or extended in front. Lift your upper chest off the floor by contracting your lower back muscles. name: Scapular Push-Up
desc: Start in a standard push-up position with arms fully extended. Without bending your elbows at all, allow your chest to drop toward the floor by letting your shoulder blades come together — this is protraction. Then push the floor away and spread your shoulder blades apart — this is retraction. Your arms stay straight the entire time. This tiny movement builds the serratus anterior and teaches shoulder blade control which is fundamental for all upper body exercises and shoulder health.

desc: Lie face down on an elevated surface like a bed or a couple of stacked yoga mats, chest hanging off the edge. Hold a resistance band anchored below you, or improvise with any weighted household object. Pull toward your lower chest driving elbows back and squeezing shoulder blades. The key advantage here is your torso is fully supported, eliminating lower back strain and isolating the upper back completely.

desc: Start in a high push-up position (straight arms, not forearms). Keeping your hips perfectly level and your core tight, lift your right hand and tap your left shoulder. Place it back down. Then lift your left hand and tap your right shoulder. The entire challenge of this exercise is preventing your hips from rotating side to side as you lift each hand. Widen your feet for an easier version, narrow them for a harder challenge.

name: Dragon Flag
desc: Lie on your back gripping something solid behind your head (a pole, heavy furniture, anything stable). Brace your entire body and lift it off the ground keeping a rigid straight line from shoulders to feet — only your shoulder blades stay on the ground. Lower your legs and body slowly toward the ground while maintaining this rigid position, stopping just before touching. desc: Get into a forearm plank position with elbows on a stability ball or folded towels if no ball. desc: Lie on your back, lower back pressed firmly into the floor. Bend your knees to 90 degrees and lift both feet off the ground so your shins are parallel to the floor. name: Copenhagen Plank
desc: Lie on your side. Place your top foot on a chair or raised surface with that leg straight. Bottom leg hangs or can rest on the chair. Push up through your top foot and elbow so your body forms a straight diagonal line. Hold this position. The Copenhagen plank is unique because it specifically targets the inner thighs and adductors — usually impossible to work without equipment — while simultaneously working the obliques and lateral core.

desc: Stand on one leg. Bend the other leg behind you and hold your ankle with the same-side hand. Now squat down on the standing leg going as deep as you can — ideally until the back knee touches the floor. Drive back up through the standing heel. name: Deep Squat Hold
desc: Lower into a full deep squat and simply hold the position. Feet can be shoulder-width with toes turned slightly out. This is a fundamental human resting position that modern sitting has made extremely difficult for most people. The deep squat hold improves ankle dorsiflexion, hip mobility, and all the soft tissues around the knee. It requires zero strength but significant mobility. Practice daily and the improvement in your squat mechanics and lower body mobility will be dramatic.

name: Prisoner Squat
desc: Stand with hands interlaced behind your head, elbows pointing outward. Perform a squat keeping your chest up and maintaining this arm position throughout. The arms-behind-head position prevents you from leaning forward with your torso — forcing your core and upper back to work harder to maintain posture. This is an excellent squat variation for improving squat mechanics and upper back posture simultaneously.

name: Split Squat
desc: Stand in a staggered stance — one foot about 2-3 feet in front of the other. Lower your back knee straight down toward the floor keeping 90% of your weight on the front leg. name: Step-Up with Knee Drive
desc: Stand in front of a step, stair, or sturdy platform. name: Glute Bridge Walk-Out
desc: Start in a glute bridge position — hips raised, feet flat on floor. From this elevated position, slowly walk your feet away from your body one step at a time while keeping your hips raised. name: Frog Pump
desc: Lie on your back. Bring the soles of your feet together so your knees fall out to the sides like a frog. Place your feet at a comfortable distance from your hips. name: Standing Hip Circle
desc: Stand on one leg for balance. Lift the other knee up in front of you, then circle it out to the side, then behind you, then back to start — drawing a large circle with your knee. Do this every single day as a warm-up and you will notice dramatic improvements in hip function within weeks.

desc: Lie on your stomach. Bend one knee bringing your heel toward your glutes — but instead of just flexing the knee, try to actively contract the hamstring to pull the heel. name: Single Leg Calf Raise
desc: Stand near a wall for balance but don't lean on it. Lift one foot slightly off the floor. Rise onto the toes of your standing foot as high as possible. Lower slowly until your heel is below the step edge if possible (or just to the floor). This single-leg version places twice the load on each calf compared to the bilateral version and also challenges ankle stability. Full range of motion is essential — all the way up and all the way down.

imgSearch: single leg calf raise exercise

name: Tuck Jump
desc: Stand with feet shoulder-width. Bend slightly at the knees then explode upward jumping as high as possible. At the peak of the jump pull both knees up toward your chest simultaneously. Land softly with knees bent to absorb impact and immediately prepare for the next jump. The tuck jump develops explosive leg power, improves vertical jump height and is one of the most demanding bodyweight conditioning exercises available.

name: Broad Jump
desc: Stand with feet shoulder-width. Swing your arms back and bend into a quarter squat. Explode forward and upward simultaneously, swinging arms forward for momentum. Jump as far forward as possible. Land softly on both feet with knees bent absorbing the impact. Walk back to start and repeat. This is a horizontal power exercise that builds explosive hip extension — the foundation of all athletic movement. It directly trains the glutes and hamstrings through their full explosive range.

imgSearch: broad jump exercise plyometric
tips: Arm swing is crucial — it adds significantly to distance

name: Skater Jump
desc: Start standing on one foot. Bend slightly and leap laterally to the opposite foot, landing on it softly with the other leg trailing behind like a speed skater. As you land, immediately push off again to the original side. Swing your arms for momentum like a speed skater. This lateral movement pattern builds lateral quickness and single-leg power while hitting the glutes from a different angle than squats or lunges. Also excellent cardiovascular conditioning.

imgSearch: skater jump exercise lateral

name: Sprint in Place
desc: Stand in place and sprint at maximum speed — arms pumping hard, knees driving up high. Go absolutely all out for 10-20 seconds like your life depends on it. Rest for 40 seconds and repeat. This sprint interval protocol is extremely effective for burning fat because it triggers excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) — you continue burning calories at an elevated rate for hours after. The all-out effort is what makes it work — 70% effort produces 30% of the results.

imgSearch: sprint in place exercise cardio

name: Crab Walk
desc: Sit on the floor with hands behind you, fingers pointing toward your feet. Lift your hips off the floor so only hands and feet are on the ground. Now walk sideways or forward and backward in this position. The crab walk hits the triceps and posterior shoulders in a way that almost nothing else does while also training the glutes and core. It looks silly but it is a legitimate full-body exercise used by athletes and in animal flow training.
ytSearch: crab walk exercise tutorial animal flow
imgSearch: crab walk exercise animal flow
tips: Keep hips up throughout — don't let them sag
Challenge: keep hips as high as possible
Walk slowly and controlled for more muscle work

name: Lateral Bound
desc: Stand on one foot. Push off explosively to the side landing on the opposite foot. Stick the landing — balance for a full second before jumping back. Unlike the skater jump which is continuous this pause forces you to control the landing and build single-leg balance alongside lateral power. The pause reveals and corrects instabilities in your ankles and hips that continuous movements hide.
ytSearch: lateral bound exercise tutorial single leg power
imgSearch: lateral bound exercise agility
tips: Stick every landing — don't bounce immediately
The pause is what makes this different from skater jumps
Gradually increase the lateral distance

name: Single-Leg Deadlift (Bodyweight)
desc: Stand on one foot. Hinge at the hip pushing your free leg back behind you while your torso lowers toward the floor. Your body should form a T at the bottom — torso parallel to floor, back leg parallel to floor. Drive through the standing heel and squeeze the glutes to return upright. This combines a hip hinge with single-leg balance, training the hamstrings and glutes while building the stabilizer muscles around the ankle, knee and hip that prevent injury.
ytSearch: single leg deadlift bodyweight tutorial balance
imgSearch: single leg deadlift bodyweight exercise
tips: Keep your back leg and torso moving as one unit — like a seesaw
Slight bend in the standing knee is fine
Touch the floor with your fingertips if balance is challenged

name: Warrior III Balance
desc: Stand on one foot. Hinge forward at the hips extending the free leg behind you and arms forward until your body forms a T shape parallel to the floor. This is a yoga-derived balance exercise that builds extraordinary single-leg stability and hip strength. The extended arms and leg simultaneously challenge your balance in the sagittal plane while activating the posterior chain. Hold as still as possible.
ytSearch: warrior 3 balance pose yoga tutorial
imgSearch: warrior 3 balance yoga pose
tips: Fix your gaze on one point on the floor — it dramatically helps balance
Arms extended forward or out to the sides both work
Engage the glutes of the standing leg

name: Single-Leg Balance Progressions
desc: Stand on one foot. Begin with eyes open — hold for 30-60 seconds. To progress: close your eyes (dramatically harder), stand on a folded towel or pillow (unstable surface), perform small arm movements while balancing, or turn your head side to side. Each progression creates a new balance challenge. Single-leg balance is fundamental for knee health, ankle stability, and athletic movement. People who can't balance on one leg are at dramatically higher injury risk during any physical activity.
ytSearch: single leg balance progressions tutorial
imgSearch: single leg balance exercise
tips: Eyes closed is 3x harder than eyes open — use it
Unstable surfaces (folded towels mats pillows) are next level
Practice this barefoot for maximum proprioceptive feedback

name: Burpee to Jump Squat Complex
desc: Perform one complete burpee. Land in a squat. Immediately jump. Land in a squat. Jump again. Land and immediately go into the next burpee. Five jumps plus five burpees is one complex. This combination string is devastating to the cardiovascular system while simultaneously building total body conditioning. The transition between exercises with no rest is what makes this compound complex so effective — it removes the natural pause between separate exercises.
ytSearch: burpee jump squat complex HIIT tutorial
imgSearch: burpee jump squat complex exercise
tips: This is supposed to be hard — that is the entire point
Focus on landing quality on every jump
Scale by doing fewer repetitions of each movement

name: Crawling Complex (Bear to Crab)
desc: Start in bear crawl position (on hands and feet, hips low). Bear crawl forward 10 steps. Then flip to crab position (hands and feet but facing up). Crab walk backward 10 steps. This is an animal flow sequence that builds full-body movement quality. The transition between front and back positions requires core stability and shoulder control that most gym exercises never develop. Excellent for athletic movement development and unconventional conditioning.
ytSearch: bear crawl crab walk complex animal flow tutorial
imgSearch: bear crawl crab walk animal flow complex
tips: Transitions between bear and crab are where most people struggle — practice those
Keep hips low in bear position
A 10x10 complex each way is a serious conditioning session

name: Couch Stretch (Quad and Hip Flexor)
desc: Get your back knee onto the floor about 6 inches from a wall or sofa. Place the foot of that knee up against the wall behind you, with your shin pressing against the wall. The front foot goes forward in a lunge position. Now try to stand your torso upright. If you cannot stay upright without significant hip pain you are extremely tight. This is considered the most important hip flexor and quad stretch for athletes and people who sit. It directly reverses the damage of sitting.
ytSearch: couch stretch tutorial hip flexor quad mobility
imgSearch: couch stretch exercise hip flexor
tips: Even 2-3 minutes each side done daily will transform your hip mobility in weeks
Pain in the knee is fine mild — acute pain is not
This stretch should feel almost impossibly tight if you sit a lot

name: 90/90 Hip Stretch
desc: Sit on the floor with one leg in front bent at 90 degrees and the other leg behind bent at 90 degrees — both legs at right angles to the body. This position simultaneously stretches external hip rotation in the front leg and internal hip rotation in the back leg. Sit tall, breathe, and slowly work your torso over the front shin. Alternate sides. The 90/90 position is one of the most comprehensive hip mobility exercises available, addressing multiple restrictions at once.
ytSearch: 90 90 hip stretch tutorial mobility
imgSearch: 90 90 hip stretch exercise
tips: Don't force the position — let gravity do the work
Sitting tall is more important than leaning forward
Do this daily for dramatic hip mobility improvements

name: Thread the Needle Stretch
desc: Start on hands and knees (quadruped position). Take one arm and 'thread' it under your body by sliding it along the floor under the opposite armpit. Your shoulder will lower to the floor and your head will rest on the cheek of that side. Hold the position breathing deeply. This targets thoracic rotation and shoulder mobility — two things that become severely restricted from desk work and overhead sports. The floor provides a natural end-range that is safe and progressive.
ytSearch: thread the needle stretch thoracic rotation tutorial
imgSearch: thread the needle stretch exercise
tips: The shoulder of the threading arm rests on the floor
Breathe into the stretch — let each exhale deepen the rotation
The stacking hand can push away from the floor to increase the stretch

name: Half-Kneeling Hip Flexor + Rotation
desc: Get into a lunge position with back knee on the floor — this is the half-kneeling position. Squeeze the glute of the back leg and push your hip forward gently. You should feel a stretch in the hip flexor of the back leg. Now add a thoracic rotation — open your opposite arm to the ceiling rotating through the mid-back. Return and repeat. This combines hip flexor mobilization with thoracic rotation in one movement making it one of the most time-efficient warm-up exercises available.
ytSearch: half kneeling hip flexor rotation stretch tutorial
imgSearch: half kneeling hip flexor stretch rotation
tips: Squeeze the back glute hard — this protects the lower back and deepens the hip flexor stretch
The rotation should come from the mid-back not the lower back
10 reps each side before any workout dramatically improves performance

name: Push-Up to Downward Dog
desc: Start in a push-up position with arms extended. Perform one push-up. At the top of the push-up, push your hips up and back into downward dog — hands and feet on the floor, hips high, forming an inverted V. Hold for 1 second feeling the stretch in your hamstrings and back. Then flow back into the push-up position and repeat. This flowing combination builds chest and shoulder strength while simultaneously stretching the posterior chain, making it one of the most time-efficient bodyweight exercises.
ytSearch: push up to downward dog tutorial flow
imgSearch: push up downward dog exercise flow
tips: Flow smoothly between positions — no jerking
In downward dog really push the floor away to stretch your back
Breathe out on the push-up effort, breathe in as you flow to downdog

name: Push-Up with Rotation (T Push-Up)
desc: Perform a standard push-up. At the top of the movement, rotate your entire body to one side — lifting one arm toward the ceiling and stacking your feet — so your body forms a T or side plank position. Hold for 1 second at the top squeezing the oblique. Rotate back to push-up position and repeat on the other side. This combines the chest and shoulder work of a push-up with the rotational core challenge of a side plank in a single dynamic movement.
ytSearch: T push up rotation tutorial core
imgSearch: T push up rotation exercise
tips: The rotation comes from the whole body — not just the arm
Hold the T position for a full second
Keep the core tight during the rotation to prevent hip dropping

name: One-Leg Push-Up
desc: Perform a standard push-up but lift one foot completely off the floor before you begin. Hold that leg elevated throughout the entire set. The reduced base of support forces your core and glutes to work harder to maintain a straight body position. You will notice your whole body has to fight rotation. This simple modification significantly increases the core demand of a regular push-up without requiring any additional strength in the pushing muscles.
ytSearch: one leg push up tutorial core challenge
imgSearch: one leg push up elevated foot
tips: Lift the foot just a few inches off the floor
The tendency is for the hip of the raised leg to drop — fight that
Alternate which leg is raised each set

name: Around the World Push-Up
desc: In a push-up position, lower yourself toward one hand, sweep your chest in a wide arc across the floor from one side to the other, then push up on the opposite side. Then sweep back the other direction. The movement traces a half-circle across the floor with your chest. This unusual movement challenges the chest, shoulders and triceps through a much wider range of motion and different angles than standard push-ups, stimulating muscle fibers that straight-line movements miss.
ytSearch: around the world push up tutorial
imgSearch: around the world push up exercise
tips: Move slowly and with control — speed defeats the purpose
The arc should be as wide as possible
This requires significant shoulder mobility — work up gradually

name: Wall Walk
desc: Start in a push-up position with feet against a wall. Walk your feet up the wall while simultaneously walking your hands toward the wall until you are in a vertical handstand position against the wall. Then walk your hands away and feet down the wall to return to the starting push-up position. This teaches you the handstand position and builds the overhead strength and body awareness needed for the wall handstand push-up. The slow controlled movement against gravity is extremely demanding on the shoulders and core.
ytSearch: wall walk tutorial handstand progression calisthenics
imgSearch: wall walk exercise handstand progression
tips: Keep your core tight throughout — no arching your lower back away from the wall
The closer your hands to the wall at the top the more vertical and harder it is
Start with just a few reps — this is exhausting

name: Forearm Balance (Dolphin Pose)
desc: Start on your forearms and knees. Interlace your fingers or place forearms parallel. Tuck your toes and lift your knees — similar to a forearm plank but with hips high. Now walk your feet toward your elbows as far as possible, pushing your hips up high. This inverted position puts significant load on your shoulders preparing them for the forearm stand and handstand. Hold the highest position you can. This intermediate inversion builds the shoulder strength and flexibility for more advanced inverted work.
ytSearch: dolphin pose forearm balance tutorial shoulders
imgSearch: dolphin pose forearm balance exercise
tips: The closer your feet get to your elbows the more inverted and harder it becomes
Push the forearms into the ground — don't just rest on them
Regular practice significantly improves shoulder flexibility

name: Pike Hold (Shoulder Endurance)
desc: Get into the downward dog position — hands and feet on the floor with hips high and body forming an inverted V. Press actively through your hands pushing the floor away. Walk your feet closer to your hands to make it more vertical and harder. Hold this position while pressing your chest toward your thighs. This isometric shoulder exercise builds endurance in the anterior deltoid and serratus anterior in the overhead position, fundamental for handstand work and pressing strength.
ytSearch: pike hold downward dog shoulder endurance tutorial
imgSearch: pike hold shoulder exercise downward dog
tips: Actively push the floor away — don't just hang in the position
The more vertical your body the harder it is
Also stretches hamstrings simultaneously

name: Standing Ab Rollout (Walkout)
desc: Stand with feet hip-width. Hinge at the hips and place your hands on the floor in front of your feet. Slowly walk your hands away from your feet until you reach a push-up position or further. Hold for 1 second then walk hands back to feet and stand up. This is the floor version of the ab wheel rollout — no equipment needed. The further you walk out the harder it becomes on your core. Your lower back must NOT arch — if it does you have gone too far.
ytSearch: standing ab walkout tutorial no equipment core
imgSearch: standing ab walkout exercise core
tips: Stop walking out the moment your lower back starts to arch
The return walk-in also challenges the core — don't rush it
You can make this easier by stopping earlier in the walkout

name: Reverse Crunch
desc: Lie on your back with arms flat on the floor at your sides. Bend your knees to 90 degrees and lift your feet off the floor so shins are parallel to the floor. This is the start position. Now curl your hips up off the floor by contracting your lower abs — your knees should move toward your face. The key is to use your abs to curl the hips up, NOT to use momentum or swing your legs. Lower slowly back to the starting position. This is far more effective than regular crunches for lower abs.
ytSearch: reverse crunch tutorial lower abs form
imgSearch: reverse crunch exercise lower abs
tips: The curling motion comes from the abs squeezing — not from leg momentum
Only your lower back and hips lift off the floor — shoulders stay down
The eccentric lowering is just as important as the curl up

name: Plank Up-Downs (Mountain Plank)
desc: Start in a high push-up position. Lower one forearm down to the floor, then the other, so you are now in a forearm plank. Then press back up one arm at a time to the high push-up position. That is one repetition. The key challenge is keeping your hips perfectly level throughout the entire movement — they will want to rock side to side as you transition between positions. Alternate which arm leads each rep.
ytSearch: plank up downs tutorial mountain climber plank
imgSearch: plank up down exercise form
tips: Hips must stay level — no rocking side to side
Alternate which arm goes down first each rep
Wider feet makes this easier — narrow feet harder

name: Hollow to Arch Rock
desc: Start in the hollow body position — lying on your back with lower back pressed to floor, arms overhead, legs raised slightly, shoulders off the ground. Hold for 1 second. Now roll onto your stomach and get into arch position — arms and legs raised behind you squeezing glutes, chest off the floor. Hold for 1 second. Roll back to hollow. This combination trains both sides of the core — front and back — while the rolling motion between them improves body awareness and core control that is fundamental in gymnastics and calisthenics.
ytSearch: hollow to arch rock tutorial gymnastics core
imgSearch: hollow arch body position gymnastics
tips: Maintain tight body position throughout the roll — no flopping
Both positions should be held for a beat — not just passed through
This is a gymnastics fundamental drill used in every program

name: Seated Leg Tuck
desc: Sit on the floor with your legs extended, hands beside your hips. Lean back slightly so your torso is at about 45 degrees. Now bring your knees toward your chest while simultaneously bringing your chest toward your knees — crunching from both ends simultaneously. Extend back to the starting position without touching the floor. Keep your balance throughout on your tailbone. This is sometimes called the boat pose crunch and it trains the entire rectus abdominis under constant tension.
ytSearch: seated leg tuck crunch tutorial abs
imgSearch: seated leg tuck exercise abs
tips: Find your balance point — it takes a moment to feel it
Don't touch the floor between reps — maintain tension throughout
Extend fully between reps for the full range of motion

name: Side-Lying Leg Raise
desc: Lie on your side with your body in a straight line. Bottom arm extended under your head for support. Top leg is straight. Raise the top leg as high as comfortable keeping it straight and parallel to the floor — do not rotate the hip to get more height. Lower slowly with control. This targets the hip abductor muscles — the gluteus medius and minimus — which are critical for knee tracking during all squatting and running movements. Most people have very weak hip abductors.
ytSearch: side lying leg raise tutorial hip abductor
imgSearch: side lying leg raise exercise form
tips: Keep the foot parallel to the floor — don't let the toe point up which causes hip rotation
Raise only as high as you can without rotating the hip
Hip abductors are often neglected — they are critical for knee health

name: Superman Pulldown
desc: Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Lift your arms chest and legs off the floor simultaneously — full superman position. Now, keeping chest and legs raised, pull your elbows back toward your hips squeezing your back muscles as if you are doing a pulldown motion. Extend arms back overhead. Lower everything to the floor. This adds a dynamic back contraction to the static superman, making it significantly more effective for upper back development.
ytSearch: superman pulldown exercise back tutorial
imgSearch: superman pulldown back exercise lying
tips: Keep chest and legs raised throughout the arm movement
Squeeze the shoulder blades together as you pull the elbows back
The pulling motion should feel like a lat pulldown but lying down

name: Back Widow
desc: Lie face down with your arms bent at 90 degrees out to your sides — like a goalpost position. Lift your arms and chest off the floor by squeezing your upper back and rear delts. At the top, try to squeeze your shoulder blades together as hard as possible. Hold for 2 seconds. Lower slowly. This exercise directly targets the muscles that pull your shoulders back — the rhomboids and rear deltoids — which are the primary muscles weakened by sitting at a desk or hunching over a phone.
ytSearch: back widow exercise tutorial rhomboids posture
imgSearch: back widow exercise posture rhomboids
tips: The squeeze at the top is where all the benefit is — hold for at least 2 seconds
Arms stay at 90 degrees throughout — like goalposts
This should burn in the upper back between the shoulder blades

name: Isometric Wall Squat
desc: Stand with your back against a smooth wall. Slide down until your thighs are parallel to the floor — exactly as if you are sitting in an invisible chair. Feet should be directly below your knees, not too close to the wall. Hold this position for as long as possible while breathing steadily. The quad burn will be intense. This isometric exercise is excellent for building quad endurance and is used by martial artists, skiers and cyclists as a fundamental conditioning drill.
ytSearch: wall squat isometric hold tutorial quad burn
imgSearch: isometric wall squat exercise form
tips: Thighs must be parallel — not higher than parallel which is easier
Feet directly below knees — not tucked under the hips
Breathe steadily — holding your breath makes it harder unnecessarily

name: Lunge with Torso Twist
desc: Step forward into a lunge and lower your back knee toward the floor. At the bottom of the lunge position, rotate your torso toward the front leg — if your right foot is forward, twist right. Bring hands together at chest height to rotate further. Return torso to center, push back to standing and step forward with the other foot. This combination builds leg strength, core rotation and hip mobility simultaneously, and is commonly used in athletic warm-ups and functional fitness.
ytSearch: lunge with twist tutorial functional fitness
imgSearch: lunge torso twist exercise form
tips: Lunge into position first then add the rotation — don't try to do both simultaneously
Rotate only as far as comfortable — don't force it
Keep your front knee tracking over your toes throughout

name: Squat to Calf Raise
desc: Perform a full bodyweight squat — all the way down to parallel or beyond. Drive up through your heels. As you reach the standing position, continue the upward momentum and rise onto your toes into a calf raise. Hold for 1 second at the top of the calf raise. Lower your heels, then immediately descend into the next squat. This combination links quad and calf training seamlessly, is time-efficient, and improves ankle dorsiflexion which helps squat depth.
ytSearch: squat to calf raise combination exercise tutorial
imgSearch: squat calf raise combination exercise
tips: The transition from squat to calf raise should be smooth and continuous
Really rise as high as you can on the calf raise — full extension
This is a great circuit finisher for lower body sessions

name: Standing Quad Stretch and Flex
desc: Stand on one foot, holding a wall for balance if needed. Bend the other knee bringing your heel toward your glute. Hold the ankle with your hand. Now, here is the active component — try to extend the knee you are holding (try to straighten it against your hand's resistance) for 5 seconds, then relax and deepen the stretch. This contract-relax technique — called PNF stretching — is significantly more effective at increasing flexibility than passive stretching alone.
ytSearch: standing quad stretch PNF contract relax
imgSearch: standing quad stretch exercise
tips: The contraction against resistance is what makes this more effective than just holding
Keep your knees together — the bent knee stays beside the standing knee
Doing this regularly will noticeably improve quad flexibility within 2 weeks

name: Standing Glute Squeeze
desc: Stand with feet hip-width apart and toes pointing slightly outward. Squeeze both glutes simultaneously as hard as you possibly can — as if you are trying to crack a walnut between them. Hold for 3 seconds. Release completely. Repeat. This sounds ridiculously simple but it serves a critical purpose: teaching the mind-muscle connection to the glutes. Many people cannot effectively activate their glutes during squats, lunges and bridges because they have never learned to contract them consciously. Master this first.
ytSearch: standing glute squeeze activation tutorial
imgSearch: standing glute squeeze activation exercise
tips: Squeeze as hard as humanly possible — the harder the better
This is about neuromuscular activation — your brain learning to fire the glutes
Do this exercise before every lower body session as activation work

name: Hamstring Walkout (Nordic Preparation)
desc: Lie on your back with both feet flat on the floor knees bent. Lift your hips into a glute bridge. From this position, slowly slide one heel away from your body along the floor, straightening that leg. As you extend the leg you will feel an intense stretch and burn in the hamstring of the working leg. Slide as far as you can control, then slide it back. This is an excellent way to build hamstring flexibility and strength simultaneously without equipment and without the extreme demands of the Nordic curl.
ytSearch: hamstring walkout exercise tutorial floor
imgSearch: hamstring walkout floor exercise
tips: On a smooth floor with socks this works best
Keep hips elevated and level throughout
The further you extend the harder it is — start conservatively

name: Pulse Lunge
desc: Step into a lunge position and lower until your back knee is just above the floor. Hold this position and perform small 2-3 inch pulses — not coming up fully, just bouncing slightly in the bottom range. Stay in the burn zone for all your reps. You will feel intense burning in the front quad and the rear glute very quickly. This technique maintains constant tension on the muscles without allowing a rest at either the top or bottom. Excellent for building quad endurance and shaping the glutes.
ytSearch: pulse lunge tutorial quad glute burn
imgSearch: pulse lunge exercise form
tips: Small pulses — you should stay near the bottom range the whole time
The burn is the point — breathe through it
Switch legs immediately for maximum conditioning effect

name: Jump Rope Simulation
desc: Stand with feet together. Simulate the jumping rope motion — hop on the balls of your feet with a small bounce alternating feet or using both feet simultaneously, while rotating your forearms in circles as if turning an imaginary rope. The key is the continuous small bouncing on the balls of your feet, not big jumps. Maintain the rhythm for the full minute. This builds calf endurance, improves foot coordination, and provides excellent cardiovascular conditioning with zero equipment.
ytSearch: jump rope simulation without rope tutorial
imgSearch: jump rope simulation exercise cardio
tips: Small bounces on the balls of your feet — not big jumps
Maintain rhythm — the moment rhythm breaks it means fatigue is setting in
Build to 2-3 minute continuous sets as conditioning improves

name: Low Squat Shuffle
desc: Lower into a squat position until your thighs are at or below parallel — and stay there. Now shuffle sideways while maintaining this lowered squat position — take 5 shuffles to the right then 5 shuffles to the left. The challenge is the constant time under tension in the squat position while the shuffling motion adds a cardiovascular element. This is used heavily in basketball and football conditioning because it mimics the defensive stance. Your inner thighs and glutes will be on fire.
ytSearch: low squat shuffle lateral exercise tutorial
imgSearch: low squat shuffle exercise lateral
tips: Stay as low as possible throughout — the moment you stand up the exercise loses its effect
Keep chest up and back straight in the low position
This is harder than it looks — start with fewer shuffles and build up

name: Standing Hip Extension
desc: Stand tall next to a wall for support. Shift your weight onto one foot. Keeping the working leg completely straight, push it back and upward behind you by contracting the glute. Do not arch your lower back to get more height — the movement should come from glute contraction only. Hold for 2 seconds at the top. Lower slowly. This is one of the most accessible glute exercises for absolute beginners and those rehabbing injuries, as it requires no bending and minimal balance challenge.
ytSearch: standing hip extension glute exercise tutorial
imgSearch: standing hip extension glute exercise
tips: The glute does the work — don't just swing the leg
Keep your standing leg slightly soft not locked
Only go as high as your glute can take you without your lower back arching

name: Marching Glute Bridge
desc: Get into a glute bridge position with hips raised and both feet flat on the floor. Maintaining the elevated hip position, slowly lift one knee up toward your chest — your foot leaves the floor — hold for 2 seconds, then lower it back down without lowering your hips. Switch legs. The challenge is keeping your hips level and elevated while you march each leg. This combines glute activation from the bridge with hip flexor and core work from the marching motion.
ytSearch: marching glute bridge tutorial core stability
imgSearch: marching glute bridge exercise
tips: Hips must stay level when you lift each leg — this is the whole challenge
Squeeze the glute of the supporting leg hard
Start with small raises if the full range is too difficult to control

name: Plank Jacks
desc: Start in a high push-up position — hands on the floor, body in a straight line. Jump both feet out wide simultaneously, then jump them back together — exactly like a jumping jack but in plank position. Maintain a rigid plank throughout with hips not rising or dropping. This cardio-core hybrid keeps your core working isometrically while your legs perform a plyometric movement, making it an extremely efficient fat-burning exercise that also builds core stability.
ytSearch: plank jacks tutorial cardio core
imgSearch: plank jacks exercise form
tips: Hips must stay level — no bouncing up and down
Faster pace = more cardio, slower = more core challenge
Can be substituted with stepping feet out and in instead of jumping for lower impact

name: Squat to Punch
desc: Stand in a shoulder-width stance. Squat down, then as you drive up through your heels throw alternating punches across your body — right punch then left punch — before squatting back down. The punching motion involves a slight torso rotation that engages the core and shoulders while the squat hits the legs and glutes. This combination exercise is popular in boxing fitness and kickboxing cardio classes for its effectiveness at keeping heart rate high.
ytSearch: squat punch exercise tutorial cardio boxing
imgSearch: squat punch exercise boxing fitness
tips: Punch across the body — not straight forward — for more core engagement
Drive up strongly from the squat before punching
Keep the pace steady and rhythmic

name: Walking Lunge
desc: Step forward with one foot into a lunge. Lower your back knee close to the floor. Instead of pushing back to the starting position, push off and step your back foot forward into the next lunge with the other leg leading. Continue walking forward in this lunge pattern. The continuous walking motion keeps tension on the legs without rest between reps and adds a balance challenge as you shift weight with each step. Best done in a hallway or outdoor space.
ytSearch: walking lunge tutorial form technique
imgSearch: walking lunge exercise form
tips: Take long steps — short steps put stress on the front knee
Keep torso upright throughout — don't lean forward
On the return journey do reverse walking lunges for variety

name: Sprawl (MMA Conditioning)
desc: Stand in an athletic stance. Drop your hands to the floor and simultaneously shoot both feet back behind you into a push-up position — this is the sprawl. Your hips drop toward the floor (like a seal position) briefly, then you jump or step your feet back in and stand up explosively. The sprawl is used in MMA and wrestling for defensive movement and is one of the most explosive and effective conditioning drills. It is similar to a burpee but faster and more explosive with less emphasis on the push-up component.
ytSearch: sprawl exercise MMA conditioning tutorial
imgSearch: sprawl exercise MMA fitness
tips: The sprawl is fast and explosive — not slow like a burpee
Hips drop toward the floor at the bottom
This is genuine high-intensity training — your heart rate will spike immediately

name: Shadow Boxing
desc: Stand in a boxing stance — feet staggered slightly wider than shoulder width, dominant foot back, hands raised to guard position. Throw combinations of punches — jabs, crosses, hooks and uppercuts — while moving your feet, bobbing and weaving. The key is constant movement: feet always moving, rotating the torso with each punch, keeping hands up when not punching. Shadow boxing improves shoulder endurance, coordination, cardiovascular fitness and burns a surprising number of calories. There is no wrong way to do it — just keep moving.
ytSearch: shadow boxing tutorial beginner boxing fitness
imgSearch: shadow boxing exercise fitness
tips: Keep your hands up when not punching — this keeps shoulders working constantly
Rotate your whole torso with each punch — not just your arm
Move your feet constantly — shuffle around like a real boxer

name: Double Under Simulation
desc: Same as jump rope simulation but with higher jumps and faster wrist circles simulating a double under — where the rope passes twice under your feet per jump. Jump higher than the regular simulation with both feet and spin your forearms faster. This builds explosive calf strength, coordination and intense cardiovascular conditioning. CrossFit athletes use double unders as a core conditioning tool. The imaginary rope version still provides all the coordination and calf benefits.
ytSearch: double under simulation jump rope tutorial
imgSearch: double under jump rope exercise
tips: Jump higher than regular single-under simulation
Spin the forearms faster — the timing is the challenge
This takes real coordination to feel natural — persist through the awkward phase

name: Ankle Hops
desc: Stand with feet hip-width. Bounce rapidly on the balls of your feet using only ankle plantar flexion — the idea is to minimize knee and hip movement and make the ankles do all the work. Stay on the balls of your feet the entire time. This rapid ankle hop drill builds explosive calf strength, strengthens the Achilles tendon (the most commonly ruptured tendon in sports) and improves running economy. Every runner and jumper should include this in their training.
ytSearch: ankle hop drill tutorial calves Achilles
imgSearch: ankle hop exercise calves
tips: Knees stay almost completely still — ankles do all the work
Stay on the balls of your feet throughout — never heel strike
Start with 20 seconds and build — the calves fatigue quickly

name: Cat-Cow Spinal Mobility
desc: Get on hands and knees with hands below shoulders and knees below hips. Breathe in and drop your belly toward the floor while lifting your head and tailbone — this is cow position. Breathe out and round your back toward the ceiling tucking chin and tailbone — this is cat position. Flow between them slowly and rhythmically following your breath. This exercise restores the natural movement of the intervertebral discs, reduces morning stiffness, and is one of the most effective spinal health exercises known to physiotherapy. Do it every morning.
ytSearch: cat cow stretch tutorial spinal mobility
imgSearch: cat cow stretch exercise spine
tips: Follow your breath — inhale into cow, exhale into cat
Move slowly and feel every vertebra moving
Do this every single morning before getting out of bed

name: Supine Spinal Twist
desc: Lie on your back. Bring one knee across your body to the opposite side letting it drop toward the floor. Extend the arm on the same side as the bent knee straight out to the side. Look in the opposite direction of your knee. Both shoulders should stay in contact with the floor. Let gravity do the work — do not force the rotation. This passive spinal rotation decompresses the facet joints, stretches the IT band and lower back, and releases tension accumulated from sitting or training.

imgSearch: supine spinal twist exercise yoga
tips: Both shoulders stay on the floor — the priority is spinal rotation not getting the knee to the floor
Let gravity provide the stretch — no forcing
Hold for at least 45 seconds — most people don't hold long enough for it to be effective

name: Chest Opener Stretch
desc: Clasp your hands behind your back with fingers interlaced. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and gently pull your hands away from your back extending your elbows. Lift your chest up and push your hips forward slightly. Look upward gently. Hold this position. This is the antidote to the hunched sitting posture — it directly stretches the pec minor and biceps that become shortened from typing and phone use, while activating the rhomboids and thoracic extensors that become inhibited.
ytSearch: chest opener stretch tutorial posture correction
imgSearch: chest opener stretch exercise posture
tips: Keep the neck relaxed — don't strain it upward
Squeeze shoulder blades together first before pulling arms back
This should feel like relief if you have been sitting — do it multiple times per day

name: Child's Pose with Lat Reach
desc: Start in child's pose — sitting back on your heels, arms extended forward on the floor. Now walk both hands to one side so you feel a deep stretch down the opposite lat and side body. Hold. Walk hands to center and then to the other side. This variation targets the latissimus dorsi — the largest muscle in the back — which becomes extremely tight in people who do any overhead work, swimming, or climbing. Tight lats restrict shoulder mobility and can pull the spine into extension causing lower back pain.
ytSearch: child's pose lat reach stretch tutorial
imgSearch: child's pose lat stretch exercise
tips: Walk hands as far to the side as possible for maximum lat stretch
Breathe into the stretch — feel the ribs expand on the stretched side
Sit hips back toward heels as much as possible for deeper stretch

name: Hip Flow (Sitting 90-90 Transitions)
desc: Sit on the floor in the 90-90 position with one leg in front and one behind both at 90 degrees. Rotate both legs to the opposite side — the front leg becomes the back and the back becomes the front — lifting both knees slightly to transition. Flow between the two sides smoothly. This is a dynamic version of the 90-90 stretch that takes your hips through their full rotational range of motion actively. It is one of the most comprehensive hip mobility exercises and is used by physical therapists, athletes and yoga practitioners alike.
ytSearch: 90 90 hip flow transition tutorial mobility
imgSearch: 90 90 hip transition flow exercise
tips: Keep the transitions smooth — imagine the legs as windshield wipers
Sit as tall as possible throughout
If the transition is very difficult use your hands to help initially

name: Wall Angel
desc: Stand with your back flat against a wall — feet about 6 inches away from it. Press your lower back, upper back, head and the backs of your arms against the wall. Raise your arms to a W position with elbows bent and backs of hands against the wall. Now slowly slide your arms up the wall like making a snow angel — extending them overhead — while keeping every contact point pressed to the wall. Return to the W position. This exercise is deceptively difficult for anyone with poor posture. If your lower back lifts off the wall or your arms lose contact, those are your mobility restrictions showing themselves. With consistent practice this single exercise corrects rounded shoulders and forward head posture better than almost anything else.
ytSearch: wall angel exercise posture correction tutorial
imgSearch: wall angel exercise posture wall
tips: Every contact point must stay on the wall throughout — lower back, upper back, head, arms

This is a diagnostic tool as much as an exercise — where you lose contact tells you where you are tight

name: Prone Cobra
desc: Lie face down with arms at your sides and palms facing down. From this position, simultaneously lift your chest off the floor, rotate your arms so palms face outward and thumbs point up toward the ceiling, pull your shoulder blades together and down, and lightly contract your glutes. Hold for 2-3 seconds at the top. Lower slowly. This full combination of movements — chest lift plus external arm rotation plus scapular retraction — activates every single postural muscle in one movement. Physiotherapists prescribe this exercise for people with chronic neck pain, upper back tension and forward head posture because it directly reverses the damage of prolonged sitting.
ytSearch: prone cobra exercise posture rehabilitation tutorial
imgSearch: prone cobra exercise back posture
tips: The external arm rotation is the key — thumbs must point up at the top
Squeeze shoulder blades together AND down — not just together
Glutes should be lightly contracted — this protects the lower back

name: Breathing Squat
desc: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Take a deep diaphragmatic breath in through your nose for 4 counts. Squat down as you breathe in — reaching the bottom of the squat just as you finish inhaling. Hold your breath for 1 count at the bottom of the squat. Breathe out through your mouth for 4 counts as you drive back up to standing — reaching the top as you finish exhaling. The breath drives the movement and the movement drives the breath. This practice comes from yoga and Eastern martial arts and serves a dual purpose — building leg strength AND training diaphragmatic breathing which reduces stress hormones, improves core stability and teaches you to breathe under physical load, which most people cannot do effectively.
ytSearch: breathing squat tutorial diaphragmatic breathing exercise
imgSearch: breathing squat exercise yoga movement
tips: The breath and movement must be synchronised — inhale down, exhale up
Breathe into your belly not your chest — put one hand on your stomach and make it rise
Start with 20 slow breathing squats as a warm-up before any session — it centres the mind and activates the legs simultaneously

This will be your starting position. Flex your hips and spine to raise your torso toward your knees. At the top of the contraction your torso should be perpendicular to the ground. Reverse the motion, going only ¾ of the way down. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Air Bike

name: Barbell Deadlift

Go back to the starting position by bending at the knees while simultaneously leaning the torso forward at the waist while keeping the back straight. name: Barbell Full Squat

Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso. Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder-width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times and maintain a straight back. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it. Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso. Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section). Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and sitting your hips back as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until you slightly touch the bench behind you. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly. Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs and extend the hips to go back to the starting position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Barbell Step Ups
desc: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) and stand upright behind an elevated platform (such as the one used for spotting behind a flat bench). This is your starting position. Place the right foot on the elevated platform. Step on the platform by extending the hip and the knee of your right leg. Use the heel mainly to lift the rest of your body up and place the foot of the left leg on the platform as well. Breathe out as you execute the force required to come up. Step down with the left leg by flexing the hip and knee of the right leg as you inhale. Return to the original standing position by placing the right foot of to next to the left foot on the initial position. Repeat with the right leg for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform with the left leg.

name: Barbell Walking Lunge
desc: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and a barbell across your upper back. Step forward with one leg, flexing the knees to drop your hips. Descend until your rear knee nearly touches the ground. Your posture should remain upright, and your front knee should stay above the front foot. Drive through the heel of your lead foot and extend both knees to raise yourself back up. Step forward with your rear foot, repeating the lunge on the opposite leg.

This will be your starting position. Initiate the movement by rapidly raising one arm to shoulder level as quickly as you can. As you let that arm drop to the starting position, raise the opposite side. Continue alternating your left and right arms, whipping the ropes up and down as fast as you can.

name: Behind Head Chest Stretch
desc: Sit upright on the floor with your partner behind you. Place your hands behind your hand, and push your elbows back as far as you can. Your partner should hold your elbows. This will be your starting position. Gently attempt to pull your elbows forward with your hands still behind your head for 10 or more seconds. Your partner should prevent your elbows from moving. Now, relax your muscles and have your partner gently pull the elbows back as far as it comfortable for you. Be sure to let your partner know when the stretch is adequate to prevent overstretching or injury.

name: Bench Dips
desc: For this exercise you will need to place a bench behind your back. With the bench perpendicular to your body, and while looking away from it, hold on to the bench on its edge with the hands fully extended, separated at shoulder width. The legs will be extended forward, bent at the waist and perpendicular to your torso. This will be your starting position. Slowly lower your body as you inhale by bending at the elbows until you lower yourself far enough to where there is an angle slightly smaller than 90 degrees between the upper arm and the forearm. Tip: Keep the elbows as close as possible throughout the movement. Forearms should always be pointing down. Using your triceps to bring your torso up again, lift yourself back to the starting position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Bench Jump
desc: Begin with a box or bench 1-2 feet in front of you. Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position. Perform a short squat in preparation for the jump; swing your arms behind you. Rebound out of this position, extending through the hips, knees, and ankles to jump as high as possible. Swing your arms forward and up. Jump over the bench, landing with the knees bent, absorbing the impact through the legs. Turn around and face the opposite direction, then jump back over the bench.

name: Bench Press - Powerlifting
desc: Begin by lying on the bench, getting your head beyond the bar if possible. Tuck your feet underneath you and arch your back. Using the bar to help support your weight, lift your shoulder off the bench and retract them, squeezing the shoulder blades together. Use your feet to drive your traps into the bench. Maintain this tight body position throughout the movement. However wide your grip, it should cover the ring on the bar. Pull the bar out of the rack without protracting your shoulders. Focus on squeezing the bar and trying to pull it apart. Lower the bar to your lower chest or upper stomach. The bar, wrist, and elbow should stay in line at all times. Pause when the barbell touches your torso, and then drive the bar up with as much force as possible. The elbows should be tucked in until lockout.

name: Bench Press - With Bands
desc: Using a flat bench secure a band under the leg of the bench that is nearest to your head. Once the band is secure, grab it by both handles and lie down on the bench. Extend your arms so that you are holding the band handles in front of you at shoulder width. Once at shoulder width, rotate your wrists forward so that the palms of your hands are facing away from you. This will be your starting position. Bring down the handles slowly until your elbow forms a 90 degree angle. Keep full control at all times. As you breathe out, bring the handles up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up. Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.

name: Bent-Arm Barbell Pullover
desc: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell using a shoulder grip width. Hold the bar straight over your chest with a bend in your arms. This will be your starting position. While keeping your arms in the bent arm position, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest. At that point, bring the barbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement. Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.

name: Bent-Arm Dumbbell Pullover
desc: Place a dumbbell standing up on a flat bench. Ensuring that the dumbbell stays securely placed at the top of the bench, lie perpendicular to the bench (torso across it as in forming a cross) with only your shoulders lying on the surface. Hips should be below the bench and legs bent with feet firmly on the floor. The head will be off the bench as well. Grasp the dumbbell with both hands and hold it straight over your chest with a bend in your arms. Both palms should be pressing against the underside one of the sides of the dumbbell. This will be your starting position. Caution: Always ensure that the dumbbell used for this exercise is secure. Using a dumbbell with loose plates can result in the dumbbell falling apart and falling on your face. While keeping your arms locked in the bent arm position, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest. At that point, bring the dumbbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement. Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.

name: Bent-Knee Hip Raise
desc: Lay flat on the floor with your arms next to your sides. Now bend your knees at around a 75 degree angle and lift your feet off the floor by around 2 inches. Using your lower abs, bring your knees in towards you as you maintain the 75 degree angle bend in your legs. Continue this movement until you raise your hips off of the floor by rolling your pelvis backward. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: At the end of the movement your knees will be over your chest. Squeeze your abs at the top of the movement for a second and then return to the starting position slowly as you breathe in. Tip: Maintain a controlled motion at all times. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Bent Over Barbell Row
desc: Holding a barbell with a pronated grip (palms facing down), bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The barbell should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position. Now, while keeping the torso stationary, breathe out and lift the barbell to you. Keep the elbows close to the body and only use the forearms to hold the weight. At the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a brief pause. Then inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Bent Over Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise With Head On Bench
desc: Stand up straight while holding a dumbbell in each hand and with an incline bench in front of you. While keeping your back straight and maintaining the natural arch of your back, lean forward until your forehead touches the bench in front of you. Let the arms hang in front of you perpendicular to the ground. The palms of your hands should be facing each other and your torso should be parallel to the floor. This will be your starting position. Keeping your torso forward and stationary, and the arms straight with a slight bend at the elbows, lift the dumbbells straight to the side until both arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you lift the weights. Caution: avoid swinging the torso or bringing the arms back as opposed to the side. After a one second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position. Repeat the recommended amount of repetitions.

desc: Select a weight and hold the handle of the low pulley with your right hand. Bend at the waist until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor. Your legs should be slightly bent with your left hand placed on your lower left thigh. Your right arm should be hanging from your shoulder in front of you and with a slight bend at the elbow. This will be your starting position. Raise your right arm, elbow slightly bent, to the side until the arm is parallel to the floor and in line with your right ear. Breathe out as you perform this step. Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position as you breathe in. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and repeat the movement with the other arm.

name: Bent Over One-Arm Long Bar Row
desc: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward. Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent. Now grab the bar with one arm just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position. Pull the bar straight up with your elbow in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement. Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Do not allow for any swinging of the torso. Only the arm should move. Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones). Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and switch arms.

Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones). Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Bosu Ball Cable Crunch With Side Bends
desc: Connect a standard handle to each arm of a cable machine, and position them in the most downward position. Grab a Bosu Ball and position it in front and center of the cable machine. Lie down on the Bosu Ball with the small of your back arched around the ball. Your rear end should be close to the floor without touching it. With both hands, reach back and grab the handle of each cable. With your feet positioned in a wide stance, extend your arms straight out in front of you and in between your knees. Your hands should be at knee level. Keep your arms straight and in-line with the upward angle of the cable. Elevate your torso in a crunching motion without dropping or bending your arms. Maintain the rigid position with your arms. Slowly descend back to the starting position with your back arched around the Bosu Ball and your abdominals elongated. Repeat the same series of movements to failure. Once you reach failure, keep your abs tight and raise your torso into plank position so your back is elevated off the Bosu Ball. Lower your arms down to your side; keep them straight. Start doing alternating side bends; reach for your heels! This finishing movement will focus on your obliques.

name: Bottoms Up
desc: Begin by lying on your back on the ground. Your legs should be straight and your arms at your side. This will be your starting position. To perform the movement, tuck the knees toward your chest by flexing the hips and knees. Following this, extend your legs directly above you so that they are perpendicular to the ground. Rotate and elevate your pelvis to raise your glutes from the floor. After a brief pause, return to the starting position.

name: Box Jump (Multiple Response)
desc: Assume a relaxed stance facing the box or platform approximately an arm's length away. Arms should be down at the sides and legs slightly bent. Using the arms to aid in the initial burst, jump upward and forward, landing with feet simultaneously on top of the box or platform. Immediately drop or jump back down to the original starting place; then repeat the sequence.

name: Box Skip
desc: You will need several boxes lined up about 8 feet apart. Begin facing the first box with one leg slightly behind the other. Drive off the back leg, attempting to gain as much height with the hips as possible. Immediately upon landing on the box, drive the other leg forward and upward to gain height and distance, leaping from the box. Land between the first two boxes with the same leg that landed on the first box. Then, step to the next box and repeat.

name: Box Squat
desc: The box squat allows you to squat to desired depth and develop explosive strength in the squat movement. Begin in a power rack with a box at the appropriate height behind you. Typically, you would aim for a box height that brings you to a parallel squat, but you can train higher or lower if desired. Begin by stepping under the bar and placing it across the back of the shoulders. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and rotate your elbows forward, attempting to bend the bar across your shoulders. Remove the bar from the rack, creating a tight arch in your lower back, and step back into position. Place your feet wider for more emphasis on the back, glutes, adductors, and hamstrings, or closer together for more quad development. Keep your head facing forward. With your back, shoulders, and core tight, push your knees and butt out and you begin your descent. Sit back with your hips until you are seated on the box. Ideally, your shins should be perpendicular to the ground. Pause when you reach the box, and relax the hip flexors. Never bounce off of a box. Keeping the weight on your heels and pushing your feet and knees out, drive upward off of the box as you lead the movement with your head. Continue upward, maintaining tightness head to toe.

Additional plates can be used to hold the dumbbells down. If more tension is needed, you can either widen the base on the floor or choke the bands. name: Brachialis-SMR
desc: Lie on your side, with your upper arm against the foam roller. The upper arm should be more or less aligned with your body, with the outside of the bicep pressed against the foam roller. Raise your hips off of the floor, supporting your weight on your arm and on your feet. Hold for 10-30 seconds, and then switch sides.

name: Bradford/Rocky Presses
desc: Sit on a Military Press Bench with a bar at shoulder level with a pronated grip (palms facing forward). Tip: Your grip should be wider than shoulder width and it should create a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm as the barbell goes down. This is your starting position. Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms. Now lower the bar down to the back of the head slowly as you inhale. Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale. Lower the bar down to the starting position slowly as you inhale. This is one repetition. Alternate in this manner until you complete the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Butt-Ups
desc: Begin a pushup position but with your elbows on the ground and resting on your forearms. Your arms should be bent at a 90 degree angle. Arch your back slightly out rather than keeping your back completely straight. Raise your glutes toward the ceiling, squeezing your abs tightly to close the distance between your ribcage and hips. The end result will be that you'll end up in a high bridge position. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Lower back down slowly to your starting position as you breathe in. Tip: Don't let your back sag downwards. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

desc: Lie flat on the floor on your back with the hands by your side and your knees bent. Your feet should be placed around shoulder width. This will be your starting position. Pushing mainly with your heels, lift your hips off the floor while keeping your back straight. Breathe out as you perform this part of the motion and hold at the top for a second. Slowly go back to the starting position as you breathe in.

name: Butterfly
desc: Sit on the machine with your back flat on the pad. Take hold of the handles. Tip: Your upper arms should be positioned parallel to the floor; adjust the machine accordingly. This will be your starting position. Push the handles together slowly as you squeeze your chest in the middle. Breathe out during this part of the motion and hold the contraction for a second. Return back to the starting position slowly as you inhale until your chest muscles are fully stretched. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Cable Chest Press
desc: Adjust the weight to an appropriate amount and be seated, grasping the handles. Your upper arms should be about 45 degrees to the body, with your head and chest up. The elbows should be bent to about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position. Begin by extending through the elbow, pressing the handles together straight in front of you. Keep your shoulder blades retracted as you execute the movement. After pausing at full extension, return to th starting position, keeping tension on the cables. You can also execute this movement with your back off the pad, at an incline or decline, or alternate hands.

name: Cable Crossover
desc: To get yourself into the starting position, place the pulleys on a high position (above your head), select the resistance to be used and hold the pulleys in each hand. Step forward in front of an imaginary straight line between both pulleys while pulling your arms together in front of you. Your torso should have a small forward bend from the waist. This will be your starting position. With a slight bend on your elbows in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon, extend your arms to the side (straight out at both sides) in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms and torso should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint. Return your arms back to the starting position as you breathe out. Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights. Hold for a second at the starting position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.

name: Cable Deadlifts
desc: Move the cables to the bottom of the towers and select an appropriate weight. Stand directly in between the uprights. To begin, squat down be flexing your hips and knees until you can reach the handles. After grasping them, begin your ascent. Driving through your heels extend your hips and knees keeping your hands hanging at your side. Keep your head and chest up throughout the movement. After reaching a full standing position, Return to the starting position and repeat.

name: Cable Hammer Curls - Rope Attachment
desc: Attach a rope attachment to a low pulley and stand facing the machine about 12 inches away from it. Grasp the rope with a neutral (palms-in) grip and stand straight up keeping the natural arch of the back and your torso stationary. Put your elbows in by your side and keep them there stationary during the entire movement. Tip: Only the forearms should move; not your upper arms. This will be your starting position. Using your biceps, pull your arms up as you exhale until your biceps touch your forearms. Tip: Remember to keep the elbows in and your upper arms stationary. After a 1 second contraction where you squeeze your biceps, slowly start to bring the weight back to the original position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Cable Hip Adduction
desc: Stand in front of a low pulley facing forward with one leg next to the pulley and the other one away. Attach the ankle cuff to the cable and also to the ankle of the leg that is next to the pulley. Now step out and away from the stack with a wide stance and grasp the bar of the pulley system. Stand on the foot that does not have the ankle cuff (the far foot) and allow the leg with the cuff to be pulled towards the low pulley. This will be your starting position. Now perform the movement by moving the leg with the ankle cuff in front of the far leg by using the inner thighs to abduct the hip. Breathe out during this portion of the movement. Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then repeat the same movement with the opposite leg.

name: Cable Incline Pushdown
desc: Lie on incline an bench facing away from a high pulley machine that has a straight bar attachment on it. Grasp the straight bar attachment overhead with a pronated (overhand; palms down) shoulder width grip and extend your arms in front of you. The bar should be around 2 inches away from your upper thighs. This will be your starting position. Keeping the upper arms stationary, lift your arms back in a semi circle until the bar is straight over your head. Breathe in during this portion of the movement. Slowly go back to the starting position using your lats and hold the contraction once you reach the starting position. Breathe out during the execution of this movement. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
